book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
In which Augustin replies to all the several statements in the letter of Petilianus, as though disputing with an adversary face to face.

title: Chapter 100

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Chapter 100.—227.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Petilianus said: "But we who are poor in spirit are not apprehensive for our wealth, but rather feel a dread of wealth.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: We, ‘as having nothing, and yet possessing all things,’ look on our soul as our wealth, and by our punishments and blood purchase to ourselves the everlasting riches of heaven.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: So again the same Lord says, ‘Whosoever shall lose his substance, shall find it again an hundred fold.’"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Augustin answered: It is not beside the purpose to inquire into the true meaning of this passage also.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For where my purpose is not interfered with by any mistake which you make, or any false impression which you convey in quoting from the Scriptures, I do not concern myself about the matter.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: It is not then written, "Whosoever shall lose his substance," but "Whosoever shall lose his life for my sake."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And the passage about substance is not, "Whosoever shall lose," but "Every one that hath forsaken;" and that not only with reference to substance of money, but many other things besides.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But you meanwhile have not lost your substance; but whether you have forsaken it, in that you so boast of poverty, I cannot say.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And if by any chance my colleague Fortunatus may know this, being in the same city with you, he never told me, because I had never asked him.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: However, even if you had done this, you have yet yourself quoted the testimony of the apostle against yourself in this very epistle which you have written: "Though I bestow all my goods to feed the poor, and though I give my body to be burned, and have not charity, it profiteth me nothing."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For if you had charity, you would not bring charges against the whole world, which knows nothing of you, and of which you know no more,—no, not even such charges as are founded on the proved offenses of the Africans.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: If you had charity, you would not picture to yourself a false unity in your calumnies, but you would learn to recognize the unity that is most clearly set forth in the words of the Lord: "even in the whole earth."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But if you did not do this, why do you boast as though you had done it?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Are you really so filled with fear of riches, that, having nothing, you possess all things?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Tell that to your colleague Crispinus, who lately bought a farm near our city of Hippo, that he might there plunge men into the lowest abyss.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Whence I too know this all too well.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: You perhaps are not aware of it, and therefore shout out in security, "We stand in fear of riches."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And hence I am surprised that that cry of yours has been allowed to pass Crispinus, so as to reach us.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For between Constantina, where you are, and Hippo, where I am, lies Calama, where he is, nearer indeed to our side, but still between us.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: I wonder, therefore, how it was that he did not first intercept this cry, and strike it back so that it should not reach to our ears; and that he did not, in opposition to you, recite in much more copious phrase a eulogy on riches.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For he not only stands in no fear of riches, but he actually loves them.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And certainly, before you utter anything about the rest, you should rehearse such views to him.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: If he makes no corrections, then we have our answer ready.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But for yourself, if it be true that you are poor, you have with you my brother Fortunatus.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: You will be more likely with such sentiments to please him, who is my colleague, than Crispinus, who is your own.
